candidates.text:
The man stood by the window, watching rain fall on the wet ground. He felt lost and sad.

“Why do you stare at the glass?” asked a voice from the kitchen.

The man turned to look at the old woman. “I am thinking,” he said.

“Thinking about what?” she asked.

“About what comes next.”

She walked over to him. “Next is a new day. The sun will rise. The rain will stop.”

He looked at her. “Will it?”

“Yes,” she said. “Everything changes.”

He nodded slowly. “Then maybe I can wait.”

They sat in silence for a while. The rain continued, but the room felt warm.

The rain fell hard. I stood by the window, watching water race down glass. A knock came at the door. I opened it to find my neighbor, old Ben. His coat was soaked.

"Hello," he said. "Can I come in? The storm is fierce."

"Of course," I replied. "Come inside."

He stepped in and shook his coat. "Thank you. The wind is howling outside."

I poured him a hot drink. "Here. It will warm you."

Ben took the cup. "This is kind. The streets are flooded. My car is stuck."

"We can wait for morning," I said. "The rain will stop soon."

He nodded. "Perhaps. But I worry about my roof."

"Don't worry," I said. "I will check it with you later."

Ben smiled. "That is good news. Your kindness means much."

We sat in silence. Outside, thunder rolled. Inside, we drank warm tea and waited for the storm to pass.

The rain tapped on the roof, a soft rhythm that filled the quiet cabin. I sat by the window, watching the gray world blur outside. My old friend, Leo, stepped in, shaking water from his coat.

"Good to see you," he said, pulling a chair close. "Long time."

"Yes," I replied. "Too long."

We sat in silence for a while, listening to the storm. Then Leo spoke again. "You ever think of leaving this place? Starting fresh?"

I looked at the fire, the flames dancing low. "Maybe. But roots run deep here."

Leo nodded slowly. "True. Still, change is part of life."

I smiled faintly. "Maybe. But for now, this feels right."

The rain continued, and we stayed, two men sharing the quiet warmth of a small room.

The wind blew hard across the flat land. I sat on a wooden crate, watching the gray clouds roll in. My hands were rough, stained with oil and dirt.

"Will it rain?" asked a boy standing nearby. He wore a thin jacket.

"Yes," I said. "Soon."

The boy nodded. He looked nervous. "My mother wants us to leave."

"Go," I told him. "Take your things. Now."

He ran toward the small house. I stood up. The air grew cold. Thunder rumbled low. I grabbed my coat and moved to shelter.

A woman appeared, holding a basket. "You should go too," she said. Her voice shook.

"I will," I replied. "But first, help me close the gate."

We walked together. The wind grew stronger. Rain began to fall, hard and fast. We reached the barn just as the sky opened up.

"Thank you," the woman said, wiping water from her face.

"You’re welcome," I said. "Now rest."

We sat in silence. The storm passed quickly. Sunlight returned. The boy came running out of the house. "It’s over!" he cried.

"Good," I said. "Now we can breathe again."
